title: Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Investigating Water Quality Through Mātauranga Māori and Western Science / Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku: Te Tūhura i te Kounga Wai mā te Mātauranga Māori me te Pūtaiao Pākehā
subject: Science
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our college grounds and holds significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The awa is named after the native tree ferns that once dominated its banks and is connected to local pūrākau about Tāne Mahuta separating Ranginui and Papatūānuku. Students will explore how traditional knowledge of water monitoring (such as observing tuna populations and water clarity) aligns with modern scientific methods.
learningObjectives: Collect and analyze water quality data from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using both scientific instruments (pH, nitrate tests, turbidity tubes) and mātauranga Māori indicators (presence of tuna, clarity for mahinga kai).
Compare and contrast Western scientific and mātauranga Māori approaches to understanding and monitoring freshwater ecosystems, identifying strengths of each knowledge system.
Design and propose a collaborative kaitiakitanga action plan for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku that integrates findings from both scientific data and cultural perspectives.
Effectively communicate investigation findings and proposed actions to whānau and community members using appropriate scientific terminology and te reo Māori kupu.

activities.title: Whakatau me te Whakawhanaungatanga: Welcoming and Connecting to the Awa
activities.description: Begin with a karakia to acknowledge the awa and its mauri. A local kaumātua shares the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and discusses its historical significance as a mahinga kai and source of ora. Students make initial observations of the stream, sketching its features and noting any signs of life or potential pollution.

activities.title: He Tūhuratanga Pūtaiao: The Scientific Investigation
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students work in small groups to collect quantitative data from designated sites along the awa. They measure pH, nitrate levels, turbidity, and temperature. They also conduct a macroinvertebrate survey (stream bug hunt) to assess ecological health, recording data systematically.

activities.title: He Tūhuratanga Mātauranga Māori: The Cultural Investigation
activities.description: Guided by a kaikōrero (speaker) from a local marae, students learn traditional methods for assessing the health of the awa. This includes observing the water's clarity (wai māori), looking for signs of tuna (eels) and kōura (crayfish), and understanding what the presence or absence of these species indicates about the wellbeing of the waterway.

activities.title: He Whakataurite: Weaving the Knowledge Systems Together
activities.description: Students analyze their collected data in a hui-style discussion. They create a comparative table or a dual-axis graph to visually represent how the scientific data (e.g., nitrate levels) correlates with the mātauranga Māori indicators (e.g., absence of kōura). They discuss the complementary strengths of each approach to building a holistic understanding.

activities.title: Te Mahere Kaitiaki: Designing an Action Plan
activities.description: Based on their findings, student groups brainstorm and design a practical kaitiakitanga action plan for the awa. Proposals might include planting harakeke and other native plants to stabilize the banks and filter runoff, organizing a community clean-up day, creating educational signage, or writing a formal report to the local council with their evidence-based recommendations.

resources: Water testing kits (pH strips, nitrate tests, turbidity tubes, thermometers), kick nets, and magnifying glasses for macroinvertebrate survey.
Local kaumātua or representative from Ngāti Kahungunu iwi to share pūrākau and mātauranga Māori pertaining to the awa.
LAWA (Land, Air, Water Aotearoa) website's 'Can I Swim Here?' and 'River Quality' data pages for comparative regional data.
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's published information on local freshwater quality and riparian planting guides.

tasks: A detailed scientific report presenting the collected water quality data in graphs and tables, with a written analysis of the findings.
A reflective journal entry comparing the methods and insights gained from the pūtaiao and mātauranga Māori investigations.
A group presentation of their proposed Kaitiakitanga Action Plan for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, delivered to an audience of peers and whānau.

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in science, Understanding about science, Participating and contributing
Living World: Ecology: Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes, both natural and human-induced.
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems: Investigate the water cycle and its dynamic relationship with land, air, and heat energy.
